sentences.grammarNote:
**기쁘다** (gippeuda) = 'to be happy / joyful' — an irregular 으 verb: 기뻐요 (the 으 drops before vowel endings).
Contrast with **행복하다** (haengbokhada) = 'to be happy/content (in life)' — a deeper, lasting state; and **즐겁다** (jeulgeobda) = 'to be enjoyable/fun.'
기쁘다 expresses an immediate burst of joy — perfect for good news or an exciting moment.
**정말** (jeongmal) = 'really/truly' — one of the most common intensifiers in Korean.
무슨 일이에요? literally means 'what is the matter?' or 'what's going on?' — 일 (il) here means 'matter / situation', not 'work'. It's a natural response to someone's excited or upset expression.

합격하다 (hapgyeokhada) = 'to pass (an exam or application)'. Its opposite is 불합격하다 (bulhapgyeokhada) = 'to fail'. Exams are a huge part of Korean life — from school 수능 (CSAT) to job qualification tests — so this word comes up often.

**정말요?** (jeongmalyo?) = 'Really?' — 정말 (really) + 요 (polite sentence-final particle). Adding 요 to an adverb turns it into a polite standalone exclamation or question.
**축하하다** (chukahada) = 'to congratulate' — 축하 (祝賀, congratulation) + 하다. Polite present: 축하해요. The noun 축하 is used in 축하 메시지 (congratulatory message) and 생일 축하해요 (happy birthday).
Alternative: **축하드려요** (chukadreoyo) — a more polite form using 드리다 instead of 하다.

자랑스럽다 (jarangseureopda) = 'to be proud'. It follows the irregular ㅂ pattern: 자랑스럽다 → 자랑스러워요 (the ㅂ changes to 우 before vowels). This applies to many adjectives: 가깝다 → 가까워요 (close), 어렵다 → 어려워요 (difficult).

sentences.base_language: And I have one more piece of good news.
sentences.target_language: 그리고 좋은 소식이 하나 더 있어요.
sentences.romanized: Geurigo joeun sosigi hana deo isseoyo.
sentences.literal: And good news one more exists.
sentences.grammarNote:
**소식** (sosik) = 'news / word / tidings' — often used for personal news: 좋은 소식 (good news), 나쁜 소식 (bad news). More formal than 뉴스 (nyuseu, news broadcast).
**하나 더** (hana deo) = 'one more' — 하나 (one, native Korean) + 더 (more). Common in requests too: 하나 더 주세요 (one more, please).
**있어요** here means 'there is / I have' — the same verb handles both existence and possession in Korean.
취직하다 (chwijikada) = 'to get a job / become employed'. The related noun is 취업 (chwieop) = employment. Finding stable employment is a major life milestone in Korea, often celebrated with the family.
다행이다 (dahaengida) expresses relief — something turned out well when it might not have: 'what a relief / thankfully / fortunately'. 정말 다행이에요 is warmer and more heartfelt than just 좋아요 (good).
